report_name: DAEMON Tools Installers Trojanized in Sophisticated Supply Chain Attack

threat_actors.name: Unidentified Chinese-speaking Threat Actor
threat_actors.description: A sophisticated, suspected Chinese-speaking threat actor responsible for the supply chain attack against DAEMON Tools. The group demonstrated a clear espionage motive by selectively targeting high-value government, scientific, and manufacturing entities after a broad initial infection.
threat_actors.goals: Espionage against high-value targets in Belarus, Russia, and Thailand.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Supply Chain
attack_patterns.description: The attackers compromised the official DAEMON Tools website to distribute legitimate-looking installers bundled with a backdoor.

attack_patterns.name: Code Signing
attack_patterns.description: The compromised binaries (DTHelper.exe, DiscSoftBusServiceLite.exe, DTShellHlp.exe) were signed with a valid digital certificate from the developer, AVB Disc Soft, to bypass security checks.

attack_patterns.name: Application Layer Protocol: DNS
attack_patterns.description: The QUIC RAT implant uses DNS as one of its protocols for command and control communication.

attack_patterns.name: Encrypted Channel: Asymmetric Cryptography
attack_patterns.description: The QUIC RAT implant uses encrypted protocols, including QUIC and HTTP/3, for C2 communication, making traffic analysis difficult.

attack_patterns.name: Process Injection
attack_patterns.description: The QUIC RAT implant injects itself into the legitimate notepad.exe process to hide its presence on the system.

attack_patterns.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Windows Command Shell
attack_patterns.description: The initial backdoor is capable of receiving and executing shell commands from the C2 server.

attack_patterns.name: System Information Discovery
attack_patterns.description: The stage 1 payload, envchk.exe, was used to profile infected systems by collecting hostname, MAC address, running processes, and installed software.

malware.name: DAEMON Tools Initial Backdoor
malware.description: An initial backdoor bundled with trojanized DAEMON Tools installers (DTHelper.exe, DiscSoftBusServiceLite.exe, DTShellHlp.exe). It contacts a C2 server and downloads a reconnaissance tool (envchk.exe).

malware.name: envchk.exe
malware.description: A stage 1 information-stealing tool downloaded by the initial backdoor. It profiles the infected system by collecting hostname, MAC address, running processes, and installed software.

malware.name: cdg backdoor
malware.description: A minimalistic second-stage backdoor deployed to selected high-value targets to deliver the final QUIC RAT payload.

malware.name: QUIC RAT
malware.description: An advanced remote access trojan deployed to high-value targets. It uses QUIC, DNS, and HTTP/3 for C2 communication, employs control flow flattening, and injects into legitimate processes like notepad.exe.

tools.name: DAEMON Tools
tools.description: Legitimate disk emulation software whose official installers (versions 12.5.0.2421 to 12.5.0.2434) were trojanized and used as the vector for a large-scale supply chain attack.

tools.name: notepad.exe
tools.description: Legitimate Windows text editor abused by QUIC RAT for process injection to hide its presence.

identities.name: AVB Disc Soft
identities.description: The developer of DAEMON Tools whose software installers and digital certificate were compromised in the supply chain attack.

identities.name: Targeted Government Organizations
identities.description: Government entities in Belarus, Russia, and Thailand were among the dozen high-value targets selected for second and third stage malware deployment.

identities.name: Targeted Scientific Organizations
identities.description: Scientific and educational institutions in Belarus, Russia, and Thailand were among the dozen high-value targets. One Russian educational institution was confirmed to have been targeted with QUIC RAT.

identities.name: Targeted Manufacturing Organizations
identities.description: Manufacturing entities in Belarus, Russia, and Thailand were among the dozen high-value targets selected for advanced payload deployment.

infrastructure.name: DAEMON Tools C2 Domain
infrastructure.description: A typosquatted C2 domain (env-check.daemontools.cc) used by the initial backdoor to receive instructions and exfiltrate reconnaissance data.

infrastructure.name: DAEMON Tools Official Website
infrastructure.description: The official website (daemon-tools.cc) was compromised to distribute trojanized installers of the DAEMON Tools software.

indicators.name: DAEMON Tools Supply Chain C2 Domain
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: DAEMON Tools Supply Chain C2 DNS Query
id: 7f5b1a3d-8c4e-4f9a-9b2d-1a2b3c4d5e6f
status: experimental
description: Detects DNS queries to the typosquatted C2 domain used in the DAEMON Tools supply chain attack.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/government-scientific-entities-hit-via-daemon-tools-supply-chain-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-06
tags:
  - attack.t1071.004
  - attack.command_and_control
level: high
logsource:
  category: dns_query
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    QueryName|contains:
      - 'env-check.daemontools.cc'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects DNS queries to the known malicious C2 domain env-check.daemontools.cc used in the DAEMON Tools supply chain attack.

indicators.name: DAEMON Tools Supply Chain Malicious Binaries
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: DAEMON Tools Supply Chain Malicious File Execution
id: 8e4c2b1a-9d5f-4a8b-8c3d-2b3c4d5e6f7g
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of malicious binaries associated with the DAEMON Tools supply chain attack.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/government-scientific-entities-hit-via-daemon-tools-supply-chain-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-06
tags:
  - attack.t1195.002
level: high
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\DTHelper.exe'
      - '\DiscSoftBusServiceLite.exe'
      - '\DTShellHlp.exe'
      - '\envchk.exe'
      - '\cdg.exe'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the execution of known malicious filenames associated with the DAEMON Tools supply chain attack, including the initial backdoors and subsequent payloads.

indicators.name: Suspicious Network Activity from Notepad
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious Network Connection from Notepad.exe
id: 9d3b1c2a-8e4f-4b9a-9c2d-3a4b5c6d7e8f
status: experimental
description: Detects network connections originating from notepad.exe, which is not expected behavior. This could indicate process injection, as seen with QUIC RAT in the DAEMON Tools supply chain attack.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/government-scientific-entities-hit-via-daemon-tools-supply-chain-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-06
tags:
  - attack.t1055
  - attack.defense_evasion
level: medium
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\notepad.exe'
    Initiated: 'true'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects network connections originating from notepad.exe. This is anomalous behavior and was observed as a TTP for QUIC RAT, which injects itself into the notepad.exe process.
